subject: ap-government
theme: The Constitution protects individual freedoms from government overreach through the Bill of Rights, which selective incorporation has extended to state governments, while the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment has driven an ongoing expansion of civil rights for historically marginalized groups.

core_concepts.title: Civil Liberties vs. Civil Rights
core_concepts.detail: Civil liberties are protections from government infringement on individual freedoms (Bill of Rights); civil rights are equal treatment guarantees requiring affirmative government action — the two concepts are distinct but often tested together on the AP exam.

core_concepts.title: Selective Incorporation & the 14th Amendment
core_concepts.detail: Selective incorporation applies most Bill of Rights protections to state governments through the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause — key cases include Gideon v. Wainwright (6th Amendment right to counsel) and McDonald v. Chicago (2nd Amendment).

core_concepts.title: Establishment & Free Exercise Clauses
core_concepts.detail: The First Amendment's Establishment Clause and Free Exercise Clause create a 'wall of separation' — Engel v. Vitale (1962) banned school-sponsored prayer; Wisconsin v. Yoder (1972) protected Amish families from compulsory education, showing how the two clauses can conflict.

core_concepts.title: Student Free Speech & Prior Restraint
core_concepts.detail: Student free speech is protected under Tinker v. Des Moines (1969) — students do not shed constitutional rights at the schoolhouse gate and symbolic speech is protected unless it causes substantial disruption. New York Times Co. v. United States (1971) established a heavy presumption against government prior restraint on press publication.

core_concepts.title: Fourth Amendment & Exclusionary Rule
core_concepts.detail: The Fourth Amendment protects against unreasonable searches and seizures — evidence obtained through unconstitutional searches is barred by the exclusionary rule, which deters police violations of Fourth Amendment protections and ensures that illegally obtained evidence cannot be used to convict defendants.

core_concepts.title: Equal Protection Scrutiny Tiers
core_concepts.detail: The Equal Protection Clause generates a three-tier system of judicial review: strict scrutiny for racial classifications (compelling interest + narrow tailoring), intermediate scrutiny for gender, and rational basis for economic regulations.

core_concepts.title: Brown, Plessy & the Civil Rights Movement
core_concepts.detail: Brown v. Board of Education (1954) overturned Plessy v. Ferguson's 'separate but equal' doctrine — the civil rights movement that followed used both litigation and civil disobedience (MLK's Letter from Birmingham Jail) to dismantle de jure segregation, leading to the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and Voting Rights Act of 1965.

key_terms.definition: Protections from government infringement on individual freedoms, found primarily in the Bill of Rights (e.g., freedom of speech, religion, due process)

The first ten amendments to the U.S. Constitution, ratified in 1791, that enumerate individual freedoms — including speech, religion, assembly, and due process — protected from federal government infringement
The First Amendment protection that prohibits the government from establishing an official religion or favoring one religion over others

The First Amendment protection that bars government from interfering with individuals' practice of their religion, subject to limits when religious conduct conflicts with a compelling government interest
The umbrella First Amendment protection covering speech, press, assembly, and petition; safeguards citizens' right to express ideas and criticize government without retaliation
The standard from Schenck v. United States (1919) permitting government to limit speech that creates an imminent and serious threat — used historically to justify wartime restrictions on speech
Government action that blocks publication or speech before it occurs; presumptively unconstitutional under cases like New York Times Co. v. United States (1971)
Nonverbal expression — such as wearing armbands, burning flags, or kneeling — that conveys a message; protected by the First Amendment under cases like Tinker v. Des Moines
The publication of false written statements that damage a person's reputation; not protected by the First Amendment when made about a public figure with actual malice
Spoken communication of false statements that damage a person's reputation; like libel, falls outside First Amendment protection when made with actual malice against a public figure
Sexually explicit material that lacks serious literary, artistic, political, or scientific value under the Miller test; obscene material falls outside First Amendment protection
Laws that retroactively criminalize conduct or increase penalties for actions taken before the law was passed; explicitly prohibited by Article I of the Constitution
A legislative act that declares a person guilty of a crime and imposes punishment without a trial; prohibited by Article I of the Constitution
A court order requiring the government to justify the detention of a prisoner; protected by Article I and serves as a key safeguard against arbitrary imprisonment
Clauses in the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments requiring government to follow fair procedures and respect fundamental rights before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property
The judicial doctrine by which the Supreme Court applies most provisions of the Bill of Rights to the states one at a time through the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause
The constitutional requirement that government follow fair procedures — such as notice and a hearing — before depriving someone of life, liberty, or property
A judicial document authorizing law enforcement to conduct a search, seizure, or arrest; required by the Fourth Amendment except in narrow exceptions like exigent circumstances
The Fourth Amendment standard requiring law enforcement to have a reasonable, fact-based belief that a crime has been committed before obtaining a warrant or making an arrest
The warnings — established in Miranda v. Arizona (1966) — that police must give suspects in custody, including the right to remain silent and the right to an attorney
The Fifth Amendment protection against being tried twice for the same offense after an acquittal or conviction
Equal treatment under the law and protection from discrimination, typically requiring government action to guarantee equal opportunity across racial, gender, and other group lines
The 1865 amendment that abolished slavery and involuntary servitude in the United States, except as punishment for a crime
The 1868 amendment guaranteeing citizenship to all persons born or naturalized in the U.S.; contains the Due Process Clause and Equal Protection Clause used to incorporate rights against the states
The Fourteenth Amendment clause requiring states to provide equal legal protections to all people within their jurisdictions
The 1870 amendment prohibiting denial of the right to vote based on race, color, or previous condition of servitude
The doctrine from Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) that permitted racial segregation in public facilities so long as the facilities were nominally equal; overturned by Brown v. Board of Education (1954)
Government-mandated separation of racial groups in public schools, transportation, and accommodations; broadly dismantled by the civil rights movement and the Civil Rights Act of 1964
Segregation imposed by law — such as Jim Crow statutes — that explicitly required separation of racial groups; ruled unconstitutional in Brown v. Board of Education
Segregation that exists in practice — through housing patterns, economic disparities, or social custom — without being mandated by law; harder to remedy through litigation
Government and institutional policies designed to increase representation of historically discriminated-against groups in education and employment; subject to strict scrutiny when based on race
A large, informal network of people and organizations working outside established institutions to bring about broad social or political change, often through protest and civil disobedience
The deliberate, nonviolent refusal to obey unjust laws as a form of political protest; practitioners openly accept legal punishment to expose injustice and build moral pressure for change
Landmark federal legislation that outlawed discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin in public accommodations, employment, and federally funded programs
Federal legislation that outlawed discriminatory voting practices like literacy tests and authorized federal oversight of elections in jurisdictions with histories of voter suppression
The 1920 amendment that prohibited denial of the right to vote based on sex, granting women constitutional voting rights nationwide

key_terms.term: Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972
key_terms.definition: Federal law prohibiting sex-based discrimination in any education program or activity receiving federal funding; transformed women's athletics and protections against sexual harassment in schools

key_terms.term: The Constitution of the United States (Bill of Rights)
key_terms.definition: AP Required Foundational Document — the supreme law of the United States; the Bill of Rights (1791) constitutes its first ten amendments and is the textual source of nearly every civil liberties claim

AP Required Foundational Document — Martin Luther King Jr.'s 1963 defense of nonviolent civil disobedience, written in response to white clergy urging patience; argues that 'one has a moral responsibility to disobey unjust laws'
AP Required Supreme Court Case — held that school-sponsored prayer in public schools violates the Establishment Clause, even when the prayer is voluntary and nondenominational
AP Required Supreme Court Case — held that compelling Amish students to attend school past 8th grade violated the Free Exercise Clause; protected religious practice from neutral state laws

AP Required Supreme Court Case — held that students wearing black armbands in protest of the Vietnam War engaged in protected symbolic speech; students 'do not shed their constitutional rights at the schoolhouse gate'
AP Required Supreme Court Case — upheld the conviction of an anti-draft pamphleteer and established the 'clear and present danger' test for restricting speech, especially during wartime

key_terms.term: New York Times Co. v. United States (1971)
key_terms.definition: AP Required Supreme Court Case — held that the government's attempt to block publication of the Pentagon Papers violated the First Amendment; established a heavy presumption against prior restraint on the press
AP Required Supreme Court Case — incorporated the Second Amendment right to keep and bear arms against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause

AP Required Supreme Court Case — held that the Sixth Amendment right to counsel is a fundamental right incorporated against the states; required states to provide attorneys to felony defendants who cannot afford one
AP Required Supreme Court Case — unanimously overturned Plessy v. Ferguson's 'separate but equal' doctrine, ruling that racially segregated public schools violate the Equal Protection Clause

exam_tip: The AP exam frequently tests civil liberties and civil rights together — always distinguish between them (civil liberties = protection FROM government; civil rights = equal treatment BY government), and know the three-tier scrutiny framework: strict scrutiny for race, intermediate for gender, rational basis for economic classifications.
